title: A New Attitude

jokes.text: The narrator calls their pissing contest a 'tinkling match'
jokes.setup: Gob and Michael are having a competitive one-upmanship conversation about who they're dating
jokes.punchline: The narrator drily labels it a 'tinkling match' — a pun on 'tinkle' (urinate) standing in for 'pissing contest'
jokes.explanation: Classic AD narrator wordplay — finds a polite euphemism that is simultaneously more and less crude than the idiom it replaces. Economy is exceptional; the humor is entirely in the word choice. Rewards attentive viewers.

jokes.text: The Julie Bowen exchange — both brothers claim to be dating someone 'famousy' and immediately suspect it's Julie Bowen
jokes.setup: Both brothers claim to be dating someone famous but won't say who
jokes.punchline: They both immediately and simultaneously suspect the other is dating Julie Bowen, turning a moment of mutual suspicion into a specific, absurd shared fixation
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalating bit. The joke pivots multiple times: first the mutual secrecy, then the shared suspicion, then the escalating denials that themselves become suspicious ('I have not SEEN Julie Bowen' — 'Well, that's a funny way to phrase it'). The legalistic phrasing callback to Clinton-era deflection adds another layer. Both characters' competitive vanity is perfectly captured. Reaches genuine 8-level craft.

jokes.text: 'Well, that's a funny way to phrase it' — Michael says 'I have not seen Julie Bowen' instead of 'I am not seeing Julie Bowen'
jokes.setup: Gob demands Michael say the words 'I'm not seeing Julie Bowen'
jokes.punchline: Michael says 'I have not seen Julie Bowen' — past tense, technically true if he's seeing her now — and Gob catches the suspicious phrasing
jokes.explanation: This is a gem of tight construction. It's a callback within the same scene that deepens the joke. The Clintonian verbal precision ('I did not have sexual relations') is the comedic template, and it works beautifully. Gob's catch 'That's a funny way to phrase it' is the perfect straight-man response. Outstanding economy — four words do all the work.

jokes.text: Gob asks Michael to pretend to be his boyfriend to get into a gay club to destroy Tony Wonder, and Michael says 'I'm going to say no, thank you. And don't you think it's a little inappropriate? I am your brother.'
jokes.setup: Gob pitches his absurd revenge plan requiring Michael to play his gay boyfriend
jokes.punchline: Michael's objection is entirely on the grounds of it being inappropriate that it's his BROTHER, not that it's a gay club or that the scheme is insane
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy — Michael accepts the premise of fake-gay-dating without comment but draws the line at incest-adjacent optics. Reveals both characters efficiently.

jokes.text: George Michael: 'I love magic, of course. I mean, my dad's a magician. Well, you're my dad, I mean, so, so you should know.'
jokes.setup: Gob invites George Michael to a magic show as a bonding moment
jokes.punchline: George Michael's awkward overexplanation of why he loves magic — talking himself in circles trying to relate to the uncle/father figure
jokes.explanation: Solid George Michael characterization — his verbal fumbling under social pressure is a reliable comedic signature. Not exceptional but true to character.

jokes.text: 'Well, it'd be sort of a, a father-son thing at a gay club.' / 'Well, technically gay night at a magic club.'
jokes.setup: Gob awkwardly frames his manipulation of George Michael as a wholesome father-son outing
jokes.punchline: George Michael's gentle correction ('technically gay night at a magic club') — a meaningless distinction that he treats as important clarification
jokes.explanation: Nice deadpan exchange. George Michael's pedantry in the face of the obvious absurdity of the situation is a good character beat. The correction is technically accurate and totally beside the point.

jokes.text: Gob asks George Michael to pretend to be his boyfriend, then adds 'And don't make that face; you look homophobic.'
jokes.setup: Gob reveals his actual plan: he wants his nephew to be his fake gay boyfriend
jokes.punchline: George Michael presumably makes a horrified face, and Gob's response is to accuse him of homophobia rather than acknowledge the obvious weirdness
jokes.explanation: Perfectly captures Gob's self-serving logic — he weaponizes progressive language to deflect from his own bizarre and inappropriate request. The accusation of homophobia when the objection is clearly 'you're my uncle' is a great character move. High character integration.

jokes.text: Gob presents a 'T' wedge as proof Tony Wonder sabotaged him. Michael says 'Jesus.' Gob replies: 'No, that would be a J. This is a T.'
jokes.setup: Gob dramatically presents a piece of evidence — a 'T' shaped wedge — as proof of Tony Wonder's guilt
jokes.punchline: Michael's 'Jesus' is an exclamation of exasperation, but Gob takes it literally as a guess at what the letter stands for and patiently corrects him: 'No, that would be a J.'
jokes.explanation: This is among the episode's best jokes and one of the cleanest executions of the 'literal response to figurative speech' structure in the series. The genius is the layered setup: (1) Gob builds up the 'T' reveal with genuine dramatic seriousness, (2) Michael's 'Jesus' is a completely natural exasperation response, (3) Gob's correction ('No, that would be a J') is delivered with total sincerity and patience, as if helping a student understand a simple distinction. It perfectly captures Gob's self-deluding logic. Maximum economy — five words demolish the scene. Highly quotable. This approaches anchor-level 9 craft.

jokes.text: Gob: 'He got the last laugh, which, in this case, happened to be the first laugh.'
jokes.setup: Gob is explaining why he's still wronged even though his sabotage attempt on Tony came first
jokes.punchline: His convoluted logic produces the oxymoron 'the last laugh, which happened to be the first laugh' — delivered with complete sincerity
jokes.explanation: A beautiful example of Gob's self-serving logic creating genuine wordplay. The line is grammatically coherent but logically incoherent, which is exactly right for the character. 'I don't understand why you can't seem to follow me on any of this' is the perfect follow-up beat — Gob is genuinely baffled by Michael's confusion.

jokes.text: Gob: 'Listen, if you insist on speaking to me like you're my wife, then don't be surprised to find my [bleep] in you and then you never hear from me again.'
jokes.setup: Michael is giving Gob sincere brotherly advice, which Gob finds patronizing
jokes.punchline: Gob's threat to treat Michael like one of his discarded sexual conquests — a deeply wrong escalation that illuminates his pathological relationship avoidance
jokes.explanation: Powerfully character-specific — this is a perfect distillation of Gob's emotional unavailability expressed through his sexual M.O. The joke is disturbing and funny simultaneously. Works especially well on rewatch when you understand how deeply this pattern defines his arc this season.

jokes.text: The episode title card: 'It's Gob's Arrested Development' — a parody of the show's own title card
jokes.setup: The standard 'Arrested Development' title card plays at the start of every episode
jokes.punchline: This episode replaces it with 'It's Gob's Arrested Development' — a meta acknowledgment that we're watching Gob's version of events AND a pun on 'arrested development' as psychological immaturity
jokes.explanation: Season 4's format of personalizing the title card to each character's episode is a meta-structural joke that works on multiple levels: the show's title is itself a diagnosis of Gob's condition, and the card acknowledges the subjective nature of each episode's narrative. High craft for a structural/formal joke. The 'It's' addition ('It's Gob's Arrested Development') carries a slightly defensive, attention-seeking quality that is perfectly Gob.

jokes.text: 'Do I seem like a clown to you? I got it. Do I seem like some kind of a clown to you?' — Gob repeating the same line twice
jokes.setup: George Michael says 'I got it' after Gob's first dramatic rhetorical question
jokes.punchline: Gob repeats the exact same rhetorical question again, as if he hadn't finished his bit and George Michael had interrupted
jokes.explanation: Good Gob characterization — he has a practiced dramatic speech that he's determined to complete regardless of whether the audience is keeping up. The repetition shows he's not actually listening. Character-true and well-structured.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'which, that night, did actually kind of resemble the Gothicassle'
jokes.setup: Gob just spent considerable effort insisting there is no such place as 'the Gothicassle'
jokes.punchline: The narrator confirms that the Gothic Castle does, in fact, kind of resemble the Gothicassle that night
jokes.explanation: The narrator's deadpan validation of George Michael's 'mishearing' perfectly undercuts Gob's certainty. The word 'actually' does enormous work — it's the narrator gently siding with George Michael against Gob. Classic AD structure of narrator as unreliable ally of the audience.

jokes.text: Gob at the Gothic Castle / gay club: 'Uncle Gob.' / 'Don't call me Unc... Actually, no, that's good, that works here.'
jokes.setup: George Michael greets his uncle normally at a gay club where Gob is pretending to be gay and in a relationship
jokes.punchline: Gob starts to correct him on formality, then realizes 'Uncle Gob' actually reinforces his cover story ('Uncle' could be read as a term of endearment in this context)
jokes.explanation: Quick, efficient character beat. Gob's mid-sentence self-correction as his scheming brain catches up to his social instincts is very well observed. The dark subtext (a gay club context for 'uncle') is left implicit, which is the right call.

jokes.text: Gob: 'I do know that your daddy wants you to get your hot, little, tight, little, hot ass right up there right away to see him.'
jokes.setup: Gob is relaying a message to George Michael that his father wants to see him
jokes.punchline: Gob delivers this completely normal message in the most inappropriately sexualized language possible — doubly awkward because he's speaking to his nephew and is performing being gay
jokes.explanation: Good cringe comedy. Gob's inability to modulate his performative gay persona around his nephew is deeply uncomfortable and therefore funny. The 'hot, little, tight, little, hot' repetition suggests he's improvising his gay affect badly.

jokes.text: Gob admits the two things he told George Michael are 'unrelated' — he brought him all the way to a gay magic club just to be 'believable arm candy bean'
jokes.setup: George Michael is furious he came all this way just because Gob told him his father was upset
jokes.punchline: Gob casually admits those two things were 'unrelated' — his genuine request was just for George Michael to be his 'arm candy bean'
jokes.explanation: Solid Gob manipulation reveal. 'Arm candy bean' is a wonderfully wrong malapropism of 'arm candy' — Gob adding 'bean' without apparent awareness. Good character-specific detail.

jokes.text: George Michael mentions Fakeblock to Gob, and Gob has clearly already heard about it — then reveals 'I wonder if my dad thinks I'm blowing him off because that's starting to take off now'
jokes.setup: Gob casually mentions Fakeblock, which George Michael is surprised he knows about
jokes.punchline: George Michael immediately pivots to wondering if his famous privacy software is the reason his dad thinks he's avoiding him — his priorities (tech startup ego) perfectly revealed
jokes.explanation: A good cross-episode callback. Works as character comedy showing George Michael's growing self-importance around Fakeblock. Better for viewers who've seen his episodes.

jokes.text: Gob licks the entry stamp off George Michael's hand, reacts: 'God, what is that inky taste?' / 'It's my stamp.' / 'I probably won't come back in.'
jokes.setup: Gob and George Michael are performing a fake breakup scene at the club
jokes.punchline: Gob licks George Michael's hand (presumably as part of the act), is confused by the inky taste, and then immediately announces he 'probably won't come back in' — prioritizing the exit stamp situation over the scene he's performing
jokes.explanation: The non-sequitur of 'I probably won't come back in' is the best part — Gob's mind leaping to completely irrelevant logistics mid-performance. Good physical absurdist beat.

jokes.text: Bystander: 'Bit my tongue, that little bitch.' Gob: 'Oh, no, he didn't.' Bystander: 'They're always doing that.' Gob: 'I mean, oh, no, he didn't.'
jokes.setup: After the fake breakup scene, a bystander at the gay club reacts with campy solidarity to George Michael 'storming off'
jokes.punchline: Gob instinctively responds with the camp 'Oh, no, he didn't' before realizing that's not how he talks, then awkwardly re-delivers it trying to lean into his cover
jokes.explanation: Sharp character beat — Gob's instinctive assimilation followed by his self-consciousness is well-timed. The correction ('I mean, oh, no, he didn't') showing him trying to get back into character is good physical comedy even in text form.

jokes.text: Gob's farewell to George Michael: 'Thank you, George Michael; honestly I owe you big-time... Now get out of here. I never want to sleep with you again. I mean, I probably will. I don't. But are we good?'
jokes.setup: Gob is wrapping up his fake breakup performance with George Michael
jokes.punchline: His 'breakup' speech escalates from genuine gratitude to 'I never want to sleep with you again' (maintaining cover), to an immediate walk-back ('I mean, I probably will'), to a vulnerable 'But are we good?' — the chaos of the fake relationship bled into the real one
jokes.explanation: A beautifully constructed bit that captures Gob's emotional messiness. The escalation from sincere gratitude to sexual claim to retraction to genuine vulnerability ('But are we good?') is perfectly paced and deeply character-specific. The 'I probably will' correction is the standout line. High quotability.

jokes.text: Gob locking everything in the club: 'Who's the dumbwaiter now?'
jokes.setup: Gob is locking every possible exit/compartment in the club to trap Tony Wonder
jokes.punchline: 'Who's the dumbwaiter now?' — a pun on the service elevator (dumbwaiter) that he apparently just locked, plus a self-congratulatory rhetorical question directed at no one
jokes.explanation: A delightful throwaway that punches above its weight. The pun on 'dumbwaiter' (the person vs. the device) combined with Gob's self-satisfied delivery of a line to no one is extremely character-true. Maximum economy — four words that do triple duty. One of the episode's more quotable lines.

jokes.text: Tony Wonder's illusion: 'I don't want to look at you, son. Just go to work. Why can't you be like your brother?' — voices from his childhood closet, then popping out of a beanbag chair: 'I'm in a chair!'
jokes.setup: Tony Wonder is building to his big illusion — emerging from a 'closet' to represent coming out, with a dramatic audio backdrop of repressive voices
jokes.punchline: He bursts out of a beanbag chair instead of a traditional closet, shouting 'I'm in a chair!' — wildly deflating the dramatic buildup
jokes.explanation: 'I'm in a chair!' is one of the episode's most quotable lines — the sudden absurdity of the punchline perfectly deflates the dramatic staging. The beanbag chair as a metaphor for the closet is both apt and ridiculous. Good physical comedy inferred from context.

jokes.text: 'Did somebody say Wonder?' Narrator: 'Nobody actually did that time, but I think he got away with it.'
jokes.setup: Tony Wonder's catchphrase is 'Did somebody say Wonder?' — triggered when someone says his name
jokes.punchline: The narrator notes that nobody actually said 'Wonder' this time, but concedes Tony 'got away with it'
jokes.explanation: Excellent running gag subversion — the narrator's gentle concession ('I think he got away with it') is perfectly calibrated. It acknowledges the cheat while forgiving it, which is both funny and generous. Great economy.

jokes.text: Narrator on the illusion: 'it really brought the crowd to their feet. Although it was pretty easy to figure out how he did it. They started out gay.'
jokes.setup: Tony Wonder's 'Now everybody's gay!' finale apparently wows the crowd
jokes.punchline: The narrator's dry explanation: 'It was pretty easy to figure out how he did it. They started out gay.'
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's sharpest jokes. The narrator's deadpan explanation ('They started out gay') demolishes the illusion with perfect economy. The structure — build up the trick, hint at revelation, deliver the anti-climactic truth — is textbook. Extremely quotable. This is the narrator at their best.

jokes.text: Tony reveals his beanbag trick: 'I put it on another guy, and then I was in the bean bag chair the whole time.' / 'Oh, well, that's... that's... that's why they put that sign on the bean bag chair that says, Do not steal this...'
jokes.setup: Magicians sharing professional secrets — Tony explains his beanbag trick
jokes.punchline: Gob begins to understand the trick and starts connecting it to a specific sign on the beanbag chair, which hilariously implies the chair was stolen from somewhere (and labeled 'Do not steal this')
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist detail — the sign 'Do not steal this' on the beanbag chair is a funny prop gag that reveals petty criminality inside the magic trick explanation. The incomplete sentence adds to the comedy.

jokes.text: Tony: 'Also it's very hard to get out of at the end, when I appear.' — his dramatic entrance is ruined by struggling with a beanbag chair
jokes.setup: Tony is explaining the mechanics of his trick
jokes.punchline: The mundane admission that the climactic reveal is physically difficult — he struggles to get out of a beanbag chair at the dramatic moment
jokes.explanation: Functional comedy — the image of a magician dramatically struggling with a beanbag chair is inherently funny. Not exceptional but lands cleanly.

jokes.text: Gob: 'I haven't seen you since your wedding, when you did the Jesus gag.' Tony: 'See, it's like a quotation mark, but with a W.' Gob: 'Oh, yeah, right... That's... I should do that.'
jokes.setup: Tony demonstrates air quotes using finger guns as 'wedding' air quotes
jokes.punchline: Tony explains his gesture (air quotes but with a 'W' for 'wedding'), and Gob — who has presumably seen air quotes his whole life — responds 'I should do that' as if Tony just invented something incredible
jokes.explanation: Good bit — Gob's genuine admiration for Tony's trivial variation on air quotes reveals both his childlike eagerness and his tendency to be impressed by presentation over substance (which defines his relationship with magic). 'Hey, don't. Okay?' is a nice follow-up beat.

jokes.text: Tony: 'I guess you could say I was a runaway groom.' Gob: 'Well, makes sense now, doesn't it?' Tony: 'What do you mean?' Gob: 'Well, I mean, you are gay, right?'
jokes.setup: Tony references his failed marriage (to Ann), calling himself a 'runaway groom'
jokes.punchline: Gob interprets this as confirmation of Tony's (fake) gayness, connecting his departure from the wedding to his being gay — both men performing identities that are false
jokes.explanation: Good layered irony — Gob is gay (as the episode reveals) and Tony is straight, but both are performing the opposite. The 'runaway groom' pun lands cleanly and Gob's confident gay-detection is ironic given the situation.

jokes.text: Tony: 'I resorted to using my own legs in the Saw the Lady in Half gag. I even shaved them. Want to feel?' Gob: 'Wow, they are smooth. Like a lady's. I think.'
jokes.setup: Tony explains he uses his own shaved legs in his magic act since he fired his assistant
jokes.punchline: Gob feels Tony's shaved legs and confirms they're smooth 'like a lady's. I think.' — the 'I think' hedging his experience with women's legs in a way that suggests uncertainty
jokes.explanation: The 'I think' is the joke — a tiny two-word hedge that implies uncertainty about women's legs, which contradicts Gob's established womanizing and foreshadows his genuine feelings for Tony. Works better on rewatch.

jokes.text: Valet announcement: 'The Cabriolet — license plate: ANUS TART.' Gob: 'That's me.'
jokes.setup: A valet announces that a car is blocking others at the club
jokes.punchline: Gob's vanity plate is 'ANUS TART' — and he claims it without any embarrassment
jokes.explanation: Perfect. 'ANUS TART' as Gob's vanity plate is a masterclass in character-specific comedy. It's the kind of plate only Gob would have — crude, attention-seeking, and probably chosen without realizing what it actually says. His casual 'That's me' confirms he has no self-awareness about it. High character integration score — this could ONLY be Gob's plate. Extremely quotable. One of the episode's biggest laughs.

jokes.text: Tobias: 'I, too, am in a relationship, but mine is purely a revenge-based deal. Plan on making this person fall in love with me and then destroying them by breaking their heart.' Gob: 'How do you make someone fall in love with you?' Tobias: 'Can you pass the mustard, please?'
jokes.setup: Gob and Tobias are both planning revenge-love schemes against celebrities
jokes.punchline: Tobias responds to the question 'How do you make someone fall in love with you?' by asking for the mustard — either not registering the question or treating it as rhetorical while focused on his meal
jokes.explanation: Good non-sequitur deflection. Tobias's 'Can you pass the mustard?' in response to an earnest question is funny partly because it reveals Tobias may not actually know the answer, and partly because both men are so self-absorbed they barely communicate. The parallel revenge plans being swapped at breakfast is also darkly funny.

jokes.text: Tobias describes his romance: 'Last night, for instance, we both got a case of the munchies, and then we went out and we stole these pies...' Narrator: 'As it turns out, they did a lot more than steal pies.'
jokes.setup: Tobias is describing an adorable evening with his revenge target — stealing pies is the innocent example he leads with
jokes.punchline: The narrator's 'they did a lot more than steal pies' — clearly implying sex, which Tobias has elided completely while going on about pottery and candy dishes
jokes.explanation: Classic AD narrator cutting through a character's obliviousness. Tobias's romanticism about the pie-stealing while the narrator points to the obvious is a good structure. The Color Me Mine pottery detail is a great specific touch.

jokes.text: Tobias: 'they remind us both of my crooked smile.' / 'Sounds like you're really into this girl.' / 'Don't call me girl, friend. No, this is purely an act of revenge, filled with white-hot hate.'
jokes.setup: Tobias describes the personalized pottery he made with his revenge target, complete with reference to 'my crooked smile'
jokes.punchline: When Gob accurately observes that Tobias sounds smitten, Tobias insists it's 'white-hot hate' — the obvious romantic investment completely invisible to him. Also 'Don't call me girl, friend' is a comma-dependent joke.
jokes.explanation: The 'Don't call me girl, friend' is a lovely comma-dependent pun — Tobias could mean 'don't call me that, friend' or he's reclaiming a feminine identifier. His self-delusion about 'white-hot hate' vs. obvious romantic feelings is a perfect Tobias beat.

jokes.text: 'Sounds like you're really into this girl.' 'Don't call me girl, friend.'
jokes.setup: Tobias says 'sounds like you're really into this girl'
jokes.punchline: Gob's correction — 'Don't call me girl, friend' — is a double pun: he objects to 'girl' (misgendering Tony), but the phrasing sounds like he's calling Tobias his 'girlfriend'
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's single best wordplay joke. 'Don't call me girl, friend' works perfectly in multiple directions: (1) Gob is correcting that Tony is male, (2) the comma-phrasing means he's calling Tobias his girlfriend, (3) it sounds like street vernacular ('don't call me girl, friend' = 'girl, please'), (4) Gob is completely unaware of all readings. Maximum economy — five words, four simultaneous meanings. Should be in an AD highlight reel.

jokes.text: Tobias: 'Oh, I have a list of men that could fill every opening you have.'
jokes.setup: Gob needs to fill the homes in Sudden Valley; Tobias offers to help with his network of sex offenders
jokes.punchline: 'Fill every opening you have' — a Tobias double-entendre that is completely innocent in context (he means the empty homes) but perfectly consistent with his unintentional pattern of gay innuendo
jokes.explanation: A reliable Tobias running gag that lands well because of its perfect naturalness — he genuinely means the real estate openings. 'Fill every opening you have' is one of his better unintentional double-entendres this episode. High character integration.

jokes.text: Gob calls Tony Wonder, who answers: 'It's Tony. I'm not here right now, because I'm right behind you.' — it's his voicemail message
jokes.setup: Gob nervously calls Tony, trying to play it cool
jokes.punchline: Tony's voicemail is a magic trick — 'I'm not here right now, because I'm right behind you' — a greeting that is charmingly corny and perfectly character-revealing
jokes.explanation: Excellent joke. A voicemail greeting that is itself a magic trick — 'I'm not here right now, because I'm right behind you' — is perfectly character-integrated. It's self-aggrandizing, charming, and faintly ridiculous. 'Made you look.' as a follow-up, then 'But how did I know you looked? Because I'm right behind you! Made you look again.' — the escalating recursion of the trick is structurally smart. One of the episode's best character jokes.

jokes.text: Gob: 'Wow, that was fast, you hot little...' — Lucille Bluth answers instead of Tony
jokes.setup: Gob just left a flirtatious voicemail for Tony and his phone immediately rings — he assumes it's Tony calling back
jokes.punchline: It's his mother, Lucille, not Tony — making his 'hot little...' greeting land on his own mother
jokes.explanation: Classic misdirection joke — the excited 'hot little...' cutting off when Lucille identifies herself. The cringe of accidentally flirting with your mother while trying to flirt with your revenge-crush is very AD. Good economy.

jokes.text: Gob recruiting workers for the wall: 'Who wants to help me build a wall... to keep Mexicans out of America?' (bleep) 'Who wants to help me build a wall for no reason?' (bleep) 'It's a different wall.'
jokes.setup: Gob is trying to recruit laborers to build his mother's wall
jokes.punchline: His first pitch ('keep Mexicans out') gets bleeped. His revised pitch ('for no reason') also gets bleeped. 'It's a different wall' — his attempt to distinguish the two walls that are clearly the same wall
jokes.explanation: Good escalating bit. The joke has three beats: the explicit pitch, the sanitized pitch that still gets bleeped (implying 'for no reason' is also unacceptable to the crowd), and Gob's defensive 'It's a different wall.' The escalation structure is clean and the political subtext adds a dark layer without being preachy.

jokes.text: Chinese man at the border: 'You should get the Chinese to build your wall. We make great walls.' / Gob: 'That would be great. How can we make that happen?' / 'I'm Chinese; I know all Chinese. I hook you up.'
jokes.setup: Gob is looking for workers to build a wall at the border
jokes.punchline: A Chinese man offers to help, self-referencing the Great Wall of China, then claims to personally know 'all Chinese' as his credential
jokes.explanation: The Great Wall reference is an amusing coincidence joke; 'I know all Chinese' as a credential is a stereotype joke that leans on its absurdity to excuse the crudeness. Functional but not especially crafted — the earned_vs_cheap score reflects that the joke relies partly on racial absurdism as a shortcut.

jokes.text: Lucille Bluth: 'Well, if I wasn't sure you were gay, I am now.' — responding to Gob telling his mother off in front of Tony Wonder on the phone
jokes.setup: Gob is on the phone with Tony and his mother keeps interrupting; he tells her to get off his back
jokes.punchline: Lucille interprets his frustrated outburst as confirmation he's gay — based on her stereotypes about how gay men talk to their mothers
jokes.explanation: Good Lucille moment — her complete conviction combined with her backward logic is very character-true. Works especially well because she's accidentally right (Gob is gay) for entirely the wrong reasons.

jokes.text: Tony Wonder planning his scheme: 'The only thing I'm better at than [sleeping with] women is pretending I'm gay.' / 'Trust me, I am all man.' / 'Give me a little leg shave?'
jokes.setup: Tony is boasting about his masculine credentials to his co-conspirator
jokes.punchline: Immediately after declaring himself 'all man,' he requests a leg shave — the juxtaposition undercutting his masculine assertion
jokes.explanation: Good ironic juxtaposition. The structure — declaration of hypermasculinity immediately followed by a request associated with femininity — is clean and well-timed. The callback to the shaved legs from his earlier trick adds an extra layer.

jokes.text: Sally Sitwell reveals she stole $100,000 from Lucille Austero to rebrand Tony as the gay magician, then adds: 'And I don't want to have to sell that closet as a sweat sauna on Craigslist.'
jokes.setup: Sally is explaining the stakes of their scheme
jokes.punchline: The bizarrely specific contingency plan of selling a closet as a 'sweat sauna on Craigslist' — a panic plan that is simultaneously very specific and makes no sense
jokes.explanation: Great absurdist specificity — 'sweat sauna on Craigslist' is funny because it's both very specific and entirely implausible as a closet use case. The desperation implied in the fallback plan adds character depth.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'And as long as we're recapping things that we already know...' — the 'inside source' reveal about Fakeblock
jokes.setup: The scene is repeating information that's been established in other episodes of Season 4
jokes.punchline: The narrator breaks form to acknowledge the show's own exposition repetition: 'And as long as we're recapping things that we already know...'
jokes.explanation: A sharp meta-joke about Season 4's structure — the overlapping multi-episode storylines meant the same information had to be repeated in multiple character episodes. The narrator acknowledging this is honest and funny, a rare moment of the show winking at its own structural challenge.

jokes.text: Tony trying not to react when he hears 'wonder': narrator notes 'It took a lot of restraint for Tony to not pop out when he heard the word wonder'
jokes.setup: Tony is hiding in the beanbag chair during Gob and George Michael's conversation; George Michael says 'I wonder if my dad thinks...'
jokes.punchline: The narrator reveals Tony is physically suppressing his 'Did somebody say Wonder?' instinct when he hears the word — a Pavlovian response he's trained himself into
jokes.explanation: A great visual comedy beat described entirely through narration. Tony straining not to pop out of a beanbag chair when he hears 'wonder' is deeply funny as a physical image. The running gag has been built well enough that this suppression of it is funnier than the gag itself would be.

jokes.text: Tony: 'Poor guy doesn't realize I'm about to destroy our life.' / 'Our life?' / 'I mean, destroy our life with Gob. Dest... I'm going to destroy our life-- my life-- with Gob.'
jokes.setup: Tony is narrating his plan to destroy Gob to Sally Sitwell
jokes.punchline: Tony's speech keeps accidentally including himself in the destruction ('our life') — his self-corrections getting more tangled as he goes: 'destroy our life — my life — with Gob'
jokes.explanation: Mirrors Gob's earlier slip ('Destroy our life') — both men are unconsciously including the other in their future while ostensibly planning revenge. The escalating self-correction is a structurally excellent comedic device that the episode uses symmetrically. Works as foreshadowing for the 'Same' sequence.

jokes.text: Tony's increasingly mangled self-correction: 'Destroy my life with Gob.' ♪ Hello darkness, my old friend... ♪ 'Destroy my life with Gob.' / Sally: 'Just be careful. Could be a house of cards.'
jokes.setup: Tony keeps correcting 'our life' and 'destroy our life with Gob' before landing on 'destroy my life with Gob'
jokes.punchline: The 'Hello darkness' music sting plays mid-correction, as if Tony's subconscious is commenting on his own speech — then Sally's 'house of cards' line is immediately followed by cards falling
jokes.explanation: The music gag ('Hello darkness') scoring Tony's emotional slip is a classic AD trick — using the soundtrack as a character beat. The 'house of cards' literal interpretation (cards fall immediately) is a good visual gag.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'It seemed Sally had two secrets.' — after she reacts with terror to a caterpillar/moth that is clearly a reference to something else
jokes.setup: Sally reacts dramatically to a 'caterpillar' and 'weird fuzzy moth in the drain'
jokes.punchline: The narrator's 'It seemed Sally had two secrets' — implying the caterpillar/moth references are metaphors for something about her identity (likely her hairpiece, a long-running AD gag)
jokes.explanation: A callback to an established running gag about Sally's wig/hairpiece. Works best for fans who remember the earlier seasons. The 'two secrets' structure adds intrigue. Somewhat opaque for casual viewers.

jokes.text: Gob asks Siri for 'the gayest' Little Ballroom. Siri hears 'grayest' both times. Gob: 'I need the GAYEST Little Ballroom!' Siri: 'Okay, Gob, try this one.'
jokes.setup: Gob needs Siri to direct him to a gay bar called 'My Little Ballroom'
jokes.punchline: Siri keeps misinterpreting 'gayest' as 'grayest,' forcing Gob to repeat himself with escalating emphasis — until Siri eventually understands and directs him to the right place
jokes.explanation: Good escalating bit with a satisfying resolution. The 'grayest/gayest' mishearing is slight enough to be plausible and the escalation is well-paced. Gob's absolute certainty about what he needs ('the GAYEST') is character-true.

jokes.text: Gob arrives at the wrong Little Ballroom — a children's ball pit venue. Michael is there.
jokes.setup: Gob's Siri directed him to 'My Little Ballroom' and he arrives expecting a gay bar
jokes.punchline: He's at a children's ball pit venue — and Michael is there, equally wrongly placed (or equally rightly placed, depending on context)
jokes.explanation: Strong visual comedy — two adult men in an unexpected children's venue, both there for very different reasons, is a great structural joke. The misdirection of 'My Little Ballroom' as a venue name is well-executed AD wordplay.

jokes.text: Gob: 'First of all, that's very dated of you, Michael. He's don't like to be called she's and I don't who this he-man is...' Michael: 'No, no, it's a she. My girlfriend. Rebel. Rebel Alley. Where is she?'
jokes.setup: Michael asks where 'she' is, meaning his girlfriend; Gob assumes Michael is looking for a man
jokes.punchline: Gob lectures Michael about gendered pronouns ('He's don't like to be called she's') while Michael is simply asking about his actual girlfriend
jokes.explanation: Gob performing progressive attitudes while entirely misreading the situation is beautifully ironic — his cover story has him so committed to gay identity that he assumes all pronoun discussions are about his people. The grammar error ('He's don't like') is also authentically Gob.

jokes.text: Michael: 'I know what you do. Mom told me you were bragging about seeing somebody in showbiz. Then I see a frozen dove in a freezer.' Gob: 'That could be anything.' Michael: 'It said Love each other on the foot band.' Gob: 'Yeah, that does sound like one of mine.'
jokes.setup: Michael is trying to catch Gob in a lie about dating Rebel Alley
jokes.punchline: Gob's progression from 'That could be anything' (defending a frozen dove) to 'Yeah, that does sound like one of mine' when told it said 'Love each other' on the foot band — a perfectly timed surrender
jokes.explanation: Excellent two-beat joke. 'That could be anything' as a defense of a frozen dove is absurdly weak; the immediate concession when the sentimental dove tag is revealed ('Yeah, that does sound like one of mine') is a great character beat — Gob's doves are recognizably his.

jokes.text: Michael: 'If I want to see bad acting, I'll go see Tobias in A Jew Comes to Dinner.'
jokes.setup: Michael is accusing Gob of pretending not to know Rebel Alley
jokes.punchline: The aside 'If I want to see bad acting, I'll go see Tobias in A Jew Comes to Dinner' — an in-universe Tobias production that is both a dig at Gob and a callback to Tobias's delusional acting career
jokes.explanation: A very funny aside that works on multiple levels: insults Gob, insults Tobias, and the in-universe play title 'A Jew Comes to Dinner' (a riff on 'Guess Who's Coming to Dinner') is a great fake title that tells you everything about Tobias's taste.

jokes.text: Rebel puts a dead dove in a bag, writes down 'species and location from which she'd acquired it' and then 'one further caution' — narrator: 'It was a merry mix-up.'
jokes.setup: Rebel found a dead dove (Gob's prop) and is handling it with methodical care
jokes.punchline: The narrator's 'It was a merry mix-up' applied to a dead dove situation is perfectly incongruous — 'merry mix-up' is a sitcom cliché phrase utterly inappropriate to this dark, strange scenario
jokes.explanation: The narrator's breezy genre-label ('merry mix-up') applied to a dead bird scenario is a great tonal mismatch joke. AD frequently uses sitcom language to describe deeply weird situations, and this is a clean execution.

jokes.text: Gob: 'If ever we were gonna have a fight, Michael, this would be the safest place to do it.' / Michael: 'Right! Let's do it!' / Gob: 'This gay bar would be the best—!' Michael: 'Let's do it!'
jokes.setup: Gob notes the children's ball pit is a 'safe' place to fight (padded, soft balls everywhere)
jokes.punchline: Michael immediately agrees and starts trying to fight, while Gob is still in explanatory mode — and Gob then calls it a 'gay bar' despite being in a children's venue
jokes.explanation: Good physical comedy beat — Michael's eagerness to immediately fight vs. Gob's explanatory mode. The 'gay bar' slip from Gob (still in character from his earlier evening) adds a layer.

jokes.text: Gob running from Michael through a ball pit: 'You'll never catch me, Michael!' / ♪ You raped and pushed and struggled / All those balls in the air... ♪ / 'You have to find me, Michael!'
jokes.setup: Gob is fleeing Michael through what is presumably a children's ball pit
jokes.punchline: The chase music that plays — 'You raped and pushed and struggled / All those balls in the air' — is perfectly absurd for the ball pit setting, and Gob's 'You HAVE to find me, Michael!' suggests he both wants to escape AND wants to be caught
jokes.explanation: The chase through the ball pit is excellent physical comedy, but the real genius is the music cue — 'all those balls in the air' applied to a literal ball pit is a perfect AD musical joke. Gob's 'You HAVE to find me' adding emotional need to a comedy chase is a great character beat. High marks across the board.

jokes.text: After the fight: Gob says 'I'm just surprisingly tired. So tired... and so not hurt.'
jokes.setup: The brothers have apparently just had a fight in a ball pit
jokes.punchline: Gob admits he's tired but feels compelled to note he is 'so not hurt' — the qualifier suggesting he is, in fact, slightly hurt and doesn't want to admit it
jokes.explanation: 'So not hurt' is a perfect Gob detail — the protest is too specific to be convincing. Good economy.

jokes.text: Michael: 'With who?' / Gob: 'With sex offenders.' — the reveal of who Gob filled the homes with
jokes.setup: Gob proudly reports he filled all of Michael's unsellable homes
jokes.punchline: 'With sex offenders.' — delivered as if this is a completely satisfactory answer
jokes.explanation: Perfect two-word punchline in the classic AD tradition. The structure is flawless: Michael's hopeful 'With who?' sets up the gut-punch 'With sex offenders.' The economy is unmatched in the episode — maximum devastation in minimum words. Gob's tone of proud accomplishment makes it even better. One of the episode's top moments.

jokes.text: Michael visits Lucille, gets a black eye; Lucille assumes it's from Gob; then discovers George Senior was on the call with Gob; the call gets passed around on hold while everyone tells each other 'you're out of the movie'
jokes.setup: Michael is dealing with the fallout of Gob filling homes with sex offenders
jokes.punchline: The 'you're out of the movie' line ricochets across family members on a chain of phone calls
jokes.explanation: The chain-call structure with 'you're out of the movie' bouncing between family members is a very AD structural joke. Timing and escalation work well together. The phrase itself becoming a Bluth family currency is the meta-joke.

jokes.text: Michael goes back to Lucille and while relaying the story, George, Sr. calls in on the phone — 'I beg your pardon? Did you say George? Is that Dad? 'Cause he's the one who told me to hire Gob.'
jokes.setup: Michael is venting to his mother about Gob; she accidentally says 'George' instead of 'Gob'
jokes.punchline: Michael realizes George Sr. is on the phone — and immediately turns the blame to him, with Lucille's attempt to put him off ('Hold on, George') revealing she's been talking to both simultaneously
jokes.explanation: Good escalating comedy of errors — the phone call reveal and Michael immediately redirecting his anger is well-timed. Less exceptional but moves the scene efficiently.

jokes.text: Tony to Gob: 'Or is it because your boyfriend is 20 and I'm nearly twice that?' / Gob: 'You think that I care that you're... three years older than me?'
jokes.setup: Tony is insecure about being stood up, suggesting it's because of his age relative to Gob's 'younger boyfriend'
jokes.punchline: Gob's correction of the age difference — 'three years' instead of 'nearly twice' — is both the wrong math AND suggests Gob has very specific knowledge of Tony's age that implies interest
jokes.explanation: A good character beat — Gob's specific knowledge of Tony's age while claiming not to care reveals suppressed interest. 'Three years older than me' is also likely inaccurate math played straight.

jokes.text: Gob says his younger brother is '42' — Tony: 'Same! My younger brother's 42, also.' — then both realize and say '32' simultaneously, escalating into 'Same! Same! Same!'
jokes.setup: Gob mentions his younger brother; Tony asks how old, and Gob says '42'
jokes.punchline: Tony has the exact same 'younger brother' who is supposedly 42 — they simultaneously correct to 32 with escalating 'Same! Same!'s — the joke being that neither man's correction makes logical sense (how is a 32-year-old a 'younger' brother of someone close to that age?) and neither notices
jokes.explanation: The launch of the 'Same!' running gag that will define the rest of the scene. This initial exchange is structurally excellent — both men overstating ages and then correcting simultaneously — and the escalating 'Same! Same!' delivery suggests they're falling into a genuine bond rhythm. Sets up everything that follows beautifully.

jokes.text: The 'Same!' bonding sequence: 'My younger brother's 42, also.' / 'Same!' / 'I mean 32.' / 'Third...two...' / '32!' / 'Same!' / 'Same! I was gonna say it.' / 'Same?' / 'I was thinking the same.' / 'That's so weird!'
jokes.setup: Gob and Tony discover they have things in common; the 'Same!' refrain begins
jokes.punchline: Gob says his younger brother is 42 (wrong), corrects to 32, and Tony immediately agrees 32 — despite clearly not having a 32-year-old brother — and they're both so caught up in the 'Same!' game that neither notices the incoherence
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's absolute best sequences. The 'Same!' game is a perfect comedic device — two people so eager to connect that they agree to things that make no sense. The 42→32 correction where Tony immediately agrees '32! Same!' is structurally brilliant (he didn't have a 42-year-old brother to begin with). The sequence escalates perfectly into the wine/scuba diving/Captain Crunch montage. Extremely quotable, the 'Same!' running gag is the episode's comedic peak.

jokes.text: The 'Same!' montage — Gob and Tony finding they have everything in common: wine preference, childhood sayings, scuba diving, Captain Crunch
jokes.setup: The 'Same!' exchange has established a pattern; now it's applied to everything
jokes.punchline: Each new 'Same!' revelation — red wine, scuba diving, Captain Crunch — escalates the absurdity of their connection while the editing cuts faster and faster through their shared enthusiasm
jokes.explanation: The 'Same!' montage is the comedic high point of the episode and one of Season 4's most memorable sequences. It works because: (1) both men are supposedly plotting to destroy each other, (2) both are performing false identities, (3) they are genuinely connecting despite themselves, (4) the rapid-fire editing of 'Same!' 'Same!' creates its own rhythm. The payoff — 'It's, like, two same. That's in-same. Totally in-same.' — is the kind of wordplay that only works because of everything before it. Exceptional structure.

jokes.text: 'It's, like, two same. That's in-same. Totally in-same.'
jokes.setup: Gob and Tony have been saying 'Same!' in unison for an extended period
jokes.punchline: 'That's in-same' — a pun on 'insane' where 'same' replaces 'sane,' delivered with total sincerity by both men
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes and probably its most quotable line. 'That's in-same' is a pun that earns its wordplay by being organically generated — it emerges from the 'Same!' repetition rather than being imposed from outside. 'Totally in-same' as the confirmation doubles down perfectly. The joke rewards the audience for having sat through the escalating 'Same!' sequence. This is the payoff the whole bit has been building to.

jokes.text: Gob: 'I think I got into misdirection 'cause I just... maybe I was trying to hide my feelings or something.' Tony: 'I was just doing it to get out of gym.' Gob: 'Who's Jim?'
jokes.setup: Both men are sharing surprisingly intimate personal revelations about why they got into magic
jokes.punchline: Gob's genuine emotional revelation is immediately deflated by Tony's banal reason (avoiding gym class), then Gob mishears 'gym' as a person's name 'Jim' — revealing both his emotional connection to the moment and his literal-mindedness
jokes.explanation: A beautifully structured three-beat joke. Beat 1: Gob's vulnerable emotional revelation (hiding feelings). Beat 2: Tony's complete deflation of the moment (avoiding gym). Beat 3: Gob's mishearing ('Who's Jim?') that both misses the deflation AND shows he was emotionally invested in the conversation. 'Who's Jim?' is extremely quotable and perfectly reveals Gob's character.

jokes.text: The popcorn fight: 'Ooh! / Oh... / Don't stop. / Oh! / Popcorn fight!'
jokes.setup: Two men who are supposed to be plotting revenge against each other are instead having a spontaneous, delighted popcorn fight
jokes.punchline: 'Popcorn fight!' — the childlike joy of two grown men who were enemies finding genuine connection in the most mundane, innocent way
jokes.explanation: The comedy here is tonal — two scheming, self-serious men dissolving into a popcorn fight is deeply funny as a character beat. The 'Don't stop' line in context creates a slight double meaning that the show is comfortable leaving implicit.

jokes.text: 'It almost went down my throat.' — after the popcorn fight
jokes.setup: The popcorn fight has just ended
jokes.punchline: 'It almost went down my throat' — an innocent statement about popcorn that, in the context of two men in a gay club who are secretly attracted to each other, lands with enormous unintended weight
jokes.explanation: A perfectly placed accidental double entendre — delivered with total sincerity after the popcorn fight. The show is too smart to call attention to it, which makes it funnier. Good economy.

jokes.text: Gob sees Tony's phone and chose not to take it. Tony sees Gob's mask and chose not to sabotage it — narrator describes both men's moments of choosing not to scheme
jokes.setup: Both men had clear opportunities to advance their revenge schemes
jokes.punchline: Both independently chose not to — the narrator's 'And chose not to take it' / 'And he also chose not to' parallel structure highlighting the symmetry of their mutual abandonment of the con
jokes.explanation: A structural comedy beat — the parallel construction of both men's simultaneous moral failures (failing to con each other) is handled with elegant economy by the narrator. The comedy is in the unexpectedness of two schemers independently backing off.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'although he did put the mask down his pants. Ah.'
jokes.setup: The narrator has just praised Gob for resisting the urge to sabotage Tony
jokes.punchline: 'although he did put the mask down his pants. Ah.' — the 'Ah' being a small, resigned acknowledgment that Gob can only resist so much
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best narrator moments. The 'Ah.' at the end is doing enormous work — it's the sound of mild, resigned acknowledgment that Gob is still Gob. The structure (praise, retraction, 'Ah') is perfect. Maximum economy. 'He did put the mask down his pants' as a half-measure of evil is a brilliant character detail.

jokes.text: Gob: 'I would definitely stay and have gay sex with... or normal sex with you, but...' Tony: 'Yeah, I would totally have gay sex or whatever, but... just drank a lot of water, you know.'
jokes.setup: Both men are trying to delay the sex they'd agreed to while preserving their cover stories
jokes.punchline: Both men's parallel 'corrections' (gay sex... or normal sex) reveal their identities slipping, and both retreat to 'drank a lot of water' as their excuse — the least convincing possible reason
jokes.explanation: Exceptional character-integrated comedy. Both men's cover stories are collapsing in real time — Gob says 'gay sex... or normal sex' (slipping into heterosexual honesty), Tony says 'gay sex or whatever' (halfhearted commitment to the bit). The shared excuse of 'drank a lot of water' is perfectly absurd and structurally mirrors the 'Same!' dynamic. High marks.

jokes.text: 'I have... feelings for you.' Tony: 'I have feelings... for you.' Narrator: 'The feeling was friendship, but neither had ever experienced it.'
jokes.setup: Gob says 'I have feelings for you' — a genuine emotional breakthrough
jokes.punchline: The narrator's clarification: 'The feeling was friendship, but neither had ever experienced it' — recontextualizing a romantic declaration as a revelation of utter emotional poverty
jokes.explanation: The single best joke in the episode and one of the best narrator lines in the entire series. It works on every level: it's genuinely funny (the clarification is devastating), it's painfully true to both characters, it's structurally perfect (the pause before 'for you' is loaded, then the narrator dissects it precisely), it deepens our understanding of both men, and it's simultaneously the funniest and saddest thing in the episode. 'The feeling was friendship, but neither had ever experienced it' is the kind of line that could only exist in a show that has spent three seasons establishing exactly how emotionally stunted these men are. Near-perfect craft score.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'All they had to do now was avoid making any specific...' / Gob: 'Why don't we have normal sex on Cinco?' Tony: 'Let's have normal sex on Cinco.' Narrator: 'I guess they figured they could get out of it later. I'm not sure why they said that.'
jokes.setup: The narrator is about to warn about making specific plans
jokes.punchline: Both men immediately make a very specific plan. The narrator's 'I guess they figured they could get out of it later. I'm not sure why they said that.' — the narrator's confusion matching the audience's
jokes.explanation: Exceptional narrator comedy. The narrator cutting in to warn against specificity, being immediately overridden by the characters, then conceding confusion ('I'm not sure why they said that') is structurally brilliant. The narrator's bafflement mirrors the audience's, creating a rare moment of shared bewilderment. Also, 'normal sex on Cinco' is an absurd phrase that lands with force. One of the better narrator bits of the episode.

jokes.text: 'Why don't we have normal sex on Cinco?' / 'Let's have normal sex on Cinco.' / Narrator: 'I guess they figured they could get out of it later. I'm not sure why they said that.'
jokes.setup: The evening is ending on a warm note; both men impulsively agree to do something
jokes.punchline: They agree to 'have normal sex' — accidentally revealing neither is gay — and the narrator notes with resignation: 'I guess they figured they could get out of it later. I'm not sure why they said that.'
jokes.explanation: The narrator's bewilderment ('I'm not sure why they said that') is perfectly deployed — the show's omniscient narrator admitting he doesn't understand the characters' behavior is a very specific type of AD meta-comedy. 'Normal sex' as a slip is well-earned. The Cinco de Cuatro foreshadowing also pays off later in the episode.

jokes.text: Cinco de Cuatro — the Newport Beach 'celebration' of Cinco de Cuatro
jokes.setup: The episode transitions to Cinco de Cuatro
jokes.punchline: The running joke that Newport Beach celebrates 'Cinco de Cuatro' — May 4th — specifically to preempt Cinco de Mayo celebrations from Mexican-Americans. Referenced without explanation as established fact.
jokes.explanation: A good ongoing dark joke about the Bluth family's ambient racism. Better as accumulated lore than as a standalone joke in this episode.

jokes.text: Gob runs into an 'almost ex-wife' — 'How long has it been?' / 'It's been five years.' / 'What are you doing here?' / 'I'm taking my five-year-old to see Fantastic Four.' / 'I'm pretty sure that's an adult...'
jokes.setup: Gob runs into a woman at Cinco de Cuatro — she's been five years since they last met, she has a five-year-old
jokes.punchline: Gob's 'I'm pretty sure that's an adult...' attempting to contest the Fantastic Four movie's age appropriateness — a completely wrong deflection from the obvious implication
jokes.explanation: Good setup for what's coming — the 'five years / five-year-old' parallel is well-planted. Gob's attempt to contest the movie's rating rather than process the obvious implication is perfect character behavior.

jokes.text: 'Oui.' / 'What? We what?' / 'No, oui. It's French for yes. As in, Yes, I have a son, and his father's a magician.'
jokes.setup: The woman answers 'oui' (French for yes) to Gob's 'You have a five-year-old?'
jokes.punchline: Gob mishears 'oui' as 'we' — implying the child might be theirs together — and the woman has to explain the French, then deliver the actual punchline: 'his father's a magician'
jokes.explanation: Beautifully structured two-part joke. Part 1: the 'oui/we' mishearing lands cleanly and Gob's alarm ('What? We what?') is perfectly timed. Part 2: 'his father's a magician' — a statement that, in this episode, can only point to Tony Wonder, delivered as a revelation bomb. The structure earns its payoff through the misdirection of the French language beat.

jokes.text: Woman: 'Oh. It's not us.' Gob: 'Don't worry. It's not you. It was a real magician.'
jokes.setup: Gob seems relieved that the child might not be his
jokes.punchline: The woman insists 'it's not us' — and then explains: 'It was a real magician.' — the dig at Gob (implying he's not a 'real' magician) landing while also dropping the Tony Wonder bomb
jokes.explanation: 'It was a real magician' is a devastating line that does triple duty: (1) insults Gob, (2) reveals Tony's parenthood, (3) recontextualizes everything that came before. Economical, character-true, and punishing. 'Don't worry. It's not you.' is Gob missing the insult entirely, which makes it funnier.

jokes.text: The Gob mask / Tony Wonder mask / 'Straight bait' plan — Gob explains an increasingly convoluted plot to Ann involving masks, hidden cameras, and 'fake gay but actually straight sex'
jokes.setup: Gob wants to expose Tony as straight by filming him having straight sex while wearing a Gob mask
jokes.punchline: The plan requires Ann to wear the Gob mask, Tony to arrive expecting to sleep with 'Gob,' and then reveal she's a woman — and Gob realizes this structure is 'straight bait': 'I just realized, it's a straight bait.' 'You ever watch those?'
jokes.explanation: The plan is so convoluted it becomes its own joke. But 'straight bait' — Gob's realization mid-explanation that he's essentially designed a reverse 'To Catch a Predator' — is genuinely inspired. 'You ever watch those?' implies he's an enthusiastic viewer of those shows, adding another layer. The cascade of mask reassignments is perfectly escalating absurdism.

jokes.text: Ann: 'I have a child with him!' Gob: 'Okay, yes, new plan. I'm going to wear this Tony Wonder mask, you're going to wear the Gob mask, and... I am going to have sex with you.'
jokes.setup: Ann objects to the original plan (having sex with Tony) because she's the mother of his child
jokes.punchline: Gob's immediate pivot to an even more convoluted plan where he has sex with Ann (while they both wear masks of each other's names) — 'Okay, yes, new plan'
jokes.explanation: Great escalation. Gob's pivot from one insane plan to another even more insane plan ('Okay, yes, new plan') is perfectly Gob — he doesn't pause to question the fundamental weirdness, just adjusts the logistics. 'I am going to have sex with you' delivered after a beat as if it's the obvious solution is extremely funny.

jokes.text: Gob: 'And you know what it is? It's a straight bait. I just realized, it's a straight bait. You ever watch those?'
jokes.setup: Gob is explaining his new plan to Ann
jokes.punchline: 'It's a straight bait' — a parody of 'gay bait' (or 'straight bait' as a reversal of the entrapment show format), delivered with the satisfaction of someone who's just coined a phrase, including the 'You ever watch those?' as if this is an established genre
jokes.explanation: Gob's self-satisfied coinage of 'straight bait' (as opposed to the show's 'To Catch a Predator' format) is good character comedy — he's genuinely proud of a term that means nothing. 'You ever watch those?' as if 'straight baiting' is an established TV genre is a perfect addition.

jokes.text: Gob to Ann: 'I need a little while to get same. To be the same.'
jokes.setup: Gob is preparing himself for the encounter with Ann, asking for a few minutes
jokes.punchline: 'I need a little while to get same. To be the same.' — Gob accidentally using Tony's and his shared word 'same' as a descriptor for his mental state, revealing how deeply the evening with Tony has affected him
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best callback jokes. 'Get same' is Gob inadvertently using the shared language of his connection with Tony to describe his internal state — 'same' has replaced 'normal' or 'calm' in his vocabulary, which is both funny and quietly devastating. The self-correction ('To be the same') deepens it. Exceptional character integration — this line could ONLY be said by Gob at this exact point in the episode.

jokes.text: Tony pops up in the model home looking for somewhere to hide: 'Hello? Hello?' — and finds Ann
jokes.setup: Tony arrives early to Gob's trap, looking for a hiding spot in case someone says 'Wonder'
jokes.punchline: He finds Ann — the mother of his child, who he abandoned — in the most awkward possible reunion
jokes.explanation: A good structural joke — two people converging on the same trap location for completely different reasons. The reunion dynamic (deadbeat father meeting abandoned mother) adds dark comedy texture.

jokes.text: Tony's monologue about why David Geffen pretends to be gay, and Doug Henning being 'a secretly straight guy,' leading to: 'I mean, they can't give everything to Doogie Howser, right? Biggest fake gay there is, by the way.'
jokes.setup: Tony is explaining his strategic decision to brand as gay to Ann
jokes.punchline: His convoluted theory about gay showbiz branding arrives at 'Doogie Howser' (Neil Patrick Harris, who is actually gay) as 'the biggest fake gay there is' — spectacularly wrong
jokes.explanation: This is an excellent joke that operates on multiple levels: (1) Tony's theory is confidently, thoroughly wrong, (2) his invoking Neil Patrick Harris as evidence of 'fake gay' is spectacularly ironic since Harris is actually gay and Tony is the fake, (3) the 'Doogie Howser' name drop rather than 'Neil Patrick Harris' adds a perfectly dated quality to Tony's analysis. The self-aware absurdism here is high-quality AD.

jokes.text: 'I mean, they can't give everything to Doogie Howser, right? Biggest fake gay there is, by the way.'
jokes.setup: Tony is explaining the entertainment industry's fake-gay conspiracy
jokes.punchline: He cites Doogie Howser (Neil Patrick Harris) as 'the biggest fake gay there is' — when NPH is actually openly gay in real life, inverting the real-world facts completely
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. Neil Patrick Harris (Doogie Howser) is openly gay in real life — so calling him 'the biggest fake gay there is' inside a fiction where straight people pretend to be gay for career advancement is a beautiful inversion. The joke works on multiple levels: (1) it's funny if you don't know NPH is gay, (2) it's funnier if you do, (3) it satirizes the very idea of gayness-as-brand while deploying a real public figure. Very AD in its density.

jokes.text: Tony to Ann: 'I just thought that if you had to have sex with a man, wouldn't it be more of a turn-on to have sex with someone that looks like you?'
jokes.setup: Ann asks why she can't just be herself and have Tony wear only the Gob mask
jokes.punchline: Tony's logic: 'if you had to have sex with a man, wouldn't it be more of a turn-on to have sex with someone that looks like you?' — a narcissistic theory that also implies Ann should be attracted to herself
jokes.explanation: One of the most meta jokes in the episode — Tony's narcissism has reached the point where he believes the most erotic scenario for a woman is to have sex with someone who looks like her (i.e., Tony, wearing a Tony mask). The layers: narcissism, absurdist logic, and the inherent weirdness of the mask scenario all converge perfectly. Ann's 'Okay' is the perfect non-reaction.

jokes.text: Tony: 'Put these on, and then meet me in the bedroom in five minutes for some serious secular intercourse. With myself.'
jokes.setup: Tony is instructing Ann on the plan — they'll both wear masks and have sex
jokes.punchline: 'Serious secular intercourse. With myself.' — 'secular' targeting Ann's Christianity, while 'with myself' capturing the narcissistic logic of two people wearing each other's masks
jokes.explanation: Exceptional line. 'Serious secular intercourse' is a great phrase — 'secular' is a perfect dig at Ann's religiosity while being technically meaningless as a modifier for intercourse. 'With myself' lands as the punchline: technically true (he's wearing his own mask), narcissistically accurate, and philosophically weird. One of the episode's most quotable lines.

jokes.text: The 'Same / Same' beats score the scenes of Gob and Tony each preparing alone — 'Same. Same.' as Tony prepares, and the same word from Gob as he also prepares
jokes.setup: Both men are in separate rooms, preparing for what each believes is a trap for the other
jokes.punchline: 'Same. Same.' spoken in parallel by both men, now fully divorced from context — the word has become their emotional signature even when apart
jokes.explanation: The 'Same.' used in parallel cuts between both men preparing alone is structurally brilliant — a word that started as a comedic tic has become the emotional throughline of the episode. Their synchronized use of it while separated shows the connection is real, which is both funny (they're still trying to con each other) and genuinely poignant. High marks for structure and character integration.

jokes.text: Gob dims the lights — preparing for his assignation with Ann (wearing a Tony mask), apparently unaware he's about to have sex with the woman while both wear masks of the man each loves
jokes.setup: Gob and Tony are both about to have what they believe is elaborate revenge sex — both wearing their own and each other's masks
jokes.punchline: Narrator: 'And two men who never loved anyone but themselves... prepared to ruin each other's reputation.' — the setup for the meta-reveal that they're essentially about to have sex with each other via masks
jokes.explanation: The narrator's 'two men who never loved anyone but themselves' is perfect — it's both an indictment and, in the context of both men wearing each other's masks, literally accurate. The structural irony of their mutual narcissism accidentally creating mutual intimacy is among the episode's most sophisticated comedy writing.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'And that's how Ann not only performed a real-life version of Tony's most beloved illusion... / Now everybody's gay! / ...but was responsible for the first reported instance of a Christian straight-to-gay conversion.'
jokes.setup: The elaborate mask/sex scheme has somehow resulted in Gob and Tony both having sex believing they're with each other
jokes.punchline: The narrator's deadpan: 'the first reported instance of a Christian straight-to-gay conversion' — Ann, a devoted Christian, accidentally facilitating the thing her religion most opposes
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's second-best joke (alongside joke 81). The structure is exceptional: a callback to Tony's illusion ('Now everybody's gay!') which itself is called back by the narrator, combined with the devastating irony that Ann — the most devout Christian character on the show — is responsible for a gay conversion. The phrase 'first reported instance of a Christian straight-to-gay conversion' is both a satirical jab at conversion therapy rhetoric (reversing its direction) and a perfect character-irony punchline. This could ONLY happen in this show, with these characters.

jokes.text: Michael's long speech to Gob about feeling shame: 'If you were ashamed of being in love with a man, suddenly discovered these new feelings... tried to convince yourself that the sex you were about to have was with yourself, but it turned out to be him... then I might say something like, Homo much?'
jokes.setup: Michael has stumbled in and correctly (somehow) surmised what happened with Gob
jokes.punchline: 'then I might say something like, Homo much?' — Michael's dramatically overlong, surprisingly accurate summary of Gob's situation ends in the most reductive possible punchline
jokes.explanation: The joke is in the contrast: Michael's speech is surprisingly perceptive and emotionally articulate for Michael, only to culminate in 'Homo much?' — a jarring tonal collapse into playground-level humor. Also, Michael starts the speech with 'Bad example' and ends it still inside the 'bad example,' which is a nice structural loop.

jokes.text: Gob gives Michael the forget-me-now pill — Michael takes it, forgetting Rebel Alley; Gob remembers. Narrator: 'Stupid, forgetful Michael.'
jokes.setup: Gob offers Michael a forget-me-now (roofie) to make him forget what he saw
jokes.punchline: Michael takes it willingly (apparently) — erasing his memory of the Rebel Alley situation — while Gob retains his, leaving him with knowledge Michael doesn't have. The narrator's 'Stupid, forgetful Michael' is the topper.
jokes.explanation: A running gag payoff — the forget-me-now drug that Gob has been using to avoid emotional consequences is now used on Michael, with the ironic reversal that Gob retains the memory he'd usually want to erase. 'Stupid, forgetful Michael' is a great topper — the narrator's affectionate contempt.

jokes.text: Michael wakes up and the TV is showing an anti-abortion ad: '700,000 abortions every year...' Narrator: 'The amount of money he owed because Tobias wanted to mount this abortion.'
jokes.setup: Michael wakes up disoriented (after the forget-me-now) and turns on the TV
jokes.punchline: The narrator redefines 'abortion' from the TV ad to mean 'Tobias's terrible theatrical production' — the dual meaning landing simultaneously as a political joke and a theatrical insult
jokes.explanation: Excellent wordplay joke. The ad's '700,000 abortions every year' lands as political commentary, then the narrator immediately redefines 'abortion' in its theatrical slang sense ('a terrible production'): 'because Tobias wanted to mount this abortion.' The double meaning is well-constructed and the 'mount' adds a secondary Tobias-innuendo. Strong economy.

jokes.text: The TV plays: ♪ Fantastic Three, Fantastic... ♪ — implying Tobias's musical is called 'Fantastic Three' (not Four)
jokes.setup: Michael had told his mother he was 'pretty sure' Fantastic Four 'is an adult' movie
jokes.punchline: The TV reveals the production is actually 'Fantastic Three' — a callback to Tobias's stripped-down, budget-reduced version of the Fantastic Four, which also couldn't afford the rights to the number four
jokes.explanation: A great running gag payoff — Tobias couldn't get the rights to '4' so it's 'Fantastic Three.' The joke is funnier for being presented as casual background detail rather than being flagged. Also, the musical cutting out mid-lyric ('Fantastic Three, Fantastic...') implies even the theme song can't sustain itself.

jokes.text: Rebel to mystery caller: 'Who was that?' / 'No one.'
jokes.setup: Michael has called Rebel to come over; the scene cuts to Rebel with someone else
jokes.punchline: 'No one.' — Rebel's dismissal of Michael to whoever she's with, implying she has another relationship she's concealing. The final beat of the episode.
jokes.explanation: A classic AD episode-ending ironic kicker. Michael's emotional call ('I don't want to act like it doesn't matter to me anymore') is immediately undercut by Rebel calling him 'No one' to her other companion. Good economy — two words doing maximum damage as a cliffhanger punchline.

tmdb_overview: Gob accepts a job from his brother, but is distracted by a scheme of his own.
insight: Lindsay's thread dominates with character-driven cringe; lowest joke density of season.
